questions.question: The atomic masses of Ca and Ba are 40 and 137, respectively. According to Dobereiner’s law of triads, the atomic mass of strontium will be ________.
questions.topicName: Genesis of Periodic Classification
questions.explanation:
According to Dobereiner’s law of triads, the atomic mass of the central element is nearly the arithmetic mean of the atomic masses of the other two elements.

Ca Sr Ba Arithmetic mean
40 88 137 {tex}\frac{40+137}{2}{/tex} = 88.5 {tex}\approx{/tex} 88

questions.question: In terms of period and group, where would you locate the element with Z = 114 in the modern periodic table?

questions.explanation:
Elements with atomic numbers Z = (87 - 114) are placed in the 7th period.
Thus,  the element with Z= 114 (Flerovium) is placed in the 7th period and 14th group of the modern periodic table.

questions.explanation: Modern Periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are the periodic functions of their atomic numbers. It implies that the chemical and physical properties of the elements and any variations in these properties can easily be predicted via the outermost electronic configuration.

The period number gives an idea of Valence shell number (n) while the group number gives an idea of Valence electrons i.e. no. of electrons short to nearest octet or duplet.  
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The electronic configuration of the element in the 3rd period and 15th Group is {tex}( 1s^2 , 2s^2 2p^6 , 3s^2 3p^3 ){/tex}.

questions.question:
A natural number R is defined as R = 2a + b + 3c. Calculate the value of R if:
a = number of protons in 52 amu of He.
b = number of groups in modem periodic table.
c = number of orbitals having principal quantum number n = 4.
questions.topicName: Electronic Configurations of Elements and The Periodic Table
questions.explanation:
1 He atom = 4 amu.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 52 amu of Helium contains {tex}\left(\frac{52}{4}\right){/tex}atoms = 13 atoms.
1 atom of He contains 2 protons and 2 neutrons.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 13 atoms contain 13 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 26 protons,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 26; b = 18
The total number of orbitals having principal quantum number (n) is given as n2.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For n = 4, number of orbitals = (4)2 =16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c = 16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R = 2a + b + 3c
= (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 26) + 18 + (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) = 118

Typical metalloids have a metallic appearance, but they are brittle and only fair conductors of electricity. Chemically, they behave mostly as nonmetals. They can form alloys with metals. The general electronic configuration of different elements is given with reference to the complete configuration of an inert element preceding. It is usually denoted as {tex}[ Inert ]{/tex} and thereafter  the number of electrons in the last orbit of the element  is  written following  {tex}nl^x{/tex}  notation, where
n = principal quantum number
l = secondary or Azimuthal quantum number 
x = number of electrons
So, the general electronic configuration of group 14 elements (ie. a p-block element) is [inert] ns2, np2.

For the creation of a vacancy in the K-shell, one electron must be removed from the K-shell. Hence, electronic configuration immediately before {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} emission is: 1s2, 2s22p6, 3s23p63d10, 4s2
For the emission of {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} line the electron must jump from L-shell to K-shell. Hence, the electronic configuration immediately after emission is: 1s2, 2s22p5, 3s23p63d10, 4s2

questions.question: Ionization enthalpy increases across a period because:

options.option: outermost electrons are not held tightly.
outermost electrons are held more and more tightly.

questions.topicName: Ionization Enthalpy
questions.explanation: Consider moving from lithium to fluorine across the second period, where successive electrons are added to orbitals in the same principal quantum level. The shielding of the nuclear charge by the inner core of electrons does not increase very much to compensate for the increased attraction of the electron to the nucleus. Thus, across a period, increasing nuclear charge outweighs the shielding. Consequently, the outermost electrons are held more and more tightly and the ionization enthalpy increases across a period.

questions.question:
Characteristics/uses/occurrence of some of the elements are given below.
After identifying these elements, find which of them have the greatest difference between first and second ionization enthalpy.

options.option: Element I $\to$ Present in caustic soda, baking soda and table salt.
Element III $\to$ The second most abundant element (by mass) in the earth’s crust.
Element II $\to$ Metal present in chlorophyll of plants.
Element IV $\to$ Group 15 element that forms phosphine gas.

questions.topicName: Ionization Enthalpy
questions.explanation:
1. Element I is Sodium (Na).
  2. Element II is Magnesium (Mg).
  3. Element III is Silicon (Si).
  4. Element IV is Phosphorus (P).

Na is an alkali metal. It has only one electron in the valence shell. Therefore, its first ionization enthalpy is very low. After removal of one electron, it acquires noble gas configuration (10Ne) i.e., Na+ (1s2 2s2 2p6). So, the second ionization enthalpy is very high. Hence, the difference in first and second ionization enthalpies would be greatest in case of Na. In case of Mg, Si and P their first ionization enthalpies are much higher than that of Na but their second ionization enthalpies will be lower than that of Na. As a result, the difference in their respective ({tex}\Delta_i{/tex}H1) and ({tex}\Delta_i{/tex}H2) will be much lower than that of Na.

questions.question: The correct order of decreasing second ionization enthalpy of Ti (22), V (23), Cr (24) and Mn (25) is:
questions.topicName: Ionization Enthalpy
questions.explanation:
Second ionization enthalpy means removal of electron from unipositive ion. Electronic configuration of unipositive ions of Ti, V, Cr and Mn are as follows:
Ti+ (21) = [Ar]3d2 4s1; V+ (22) = [Ar]3d3 4s1
Cr+ (23) = [Ar]3d5 4s0; Mn+ (24) = [Ar]3d5 4s1
Now, generally, ionization energy increases with increase in atomic number but for Cr+ there is extra stability due to exactly half-filled orbital thus, it has more ionization energy than Mn+. Thus, the correct order of ionization enthalpy is Cr > Mn > V > Ti.

questions.question: Four successive members of the first row transition elements are listed below with their atomic numbers. Which one of them is expected to have the highest third ionization enthalpy?
questions.topicName: Ionization Enthalpy
questions.explanation:
Third ionization means electron is removed from dipositive ion. Electronic configuration of dipositive ions of given elements are:
V2+ (21) = [Ar]3d3 450; Cr2+ (22) = [Ar]3d4 4s0
Fe2+ (24) = [Ar]3d6 4s0; Mn2+ (23) = [Ar]3d5 4s0
Among the dipositive ions, Mn2+ has stable electronic configuration due to exactly half-filled 3d sub-shell. Hence, it requires highest energy to remove the electron and has highest third ionization enthalpy.

questions.question: Choose one of the following in the increasing order of electron gain enthalpy.
questions.topicName: Electron Gain Enthalpy
questions.explanation: In general, as we move down the group electron gain enthalpy decreases; so the expected trend should be I < Br < Cl < F  but in actual the order is I < Br < F < Cl. This is due to the small size of the F atom. The valence electrons of F atom provide strong repulsion to incoming electron, hence F has unexpectedly low electron affinity.

questions.question: Which of the following will have the most negative electron gain enthalpy and which one the least negative? P, S, Cl, F.
questions.topicName: Electron Gain Enthalpy
questions.explanation: Chlorine has the electronic configuration of 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p5, it need one more electrons to get fully filled electronic configuration. It will accept an electron easily to acquire stability, hence it has most negative electron gain enthalpy.  Whereas phosphorus has 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p3; here p-orbitals are half filled. This half filled configuration gives extra stability to phosphorus, hence it will be resistent to accept an electron. It has a positive electron affinity or in simple words energy is added to make P accept an electron.

questions.question: Among the following elements, which has the least electron affinity?
questions.topicName: Electron Gain Enthalpy
questions.explanation: Nitrogen has electronic configuration of 1s2 2s2 2p3. It has half-filled p orbitals in this outermost shell which is a stable configuration. It is difficult to add electron in such orbitals and lesser energy is released on the addition of electrons hence the electron affinity value will decrease.

The second electron affinity of oxygen is particularly high because the electron is being forced into a small, very electron-dense space. So, electron affinity is positive.

Electron gain enthalpy = -4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J/atom
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Electron affinity = 4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J/atom
Now, 1 eV = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Electron affinity
= 4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J/atom {tex}\times \frac{1 \mathrm{eV}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}}{/tex}
= 3.06 eV/atom

  • Metallic radius is always lesser than van der Waal's  radius

  Hence , the correct increasing order   of atomic radii  of an element  is covalent <  Metallic < van  der Waal's radius.

questions.question: Which one of the following molecules represents the increasing order of bond length?
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
The bond lengths depend upon the number and type of bonds present in a molecule. The greater the number of bonds shorter would be the bond length.

Thus, the given  molecules  are  all diatomic molecules where,

  1. N2 has the smallest bond length as it is triple bonded.
  2. O2 is double-bonded so its bond length is greater than N2.
  3. F2 and Cl2 both, though single-bonded, the bond length of Cl2 is greater than that of F2 due to its larger atomic size.

Hence,  the correct increasing order of given molecules is N2 < O2 < F2 < Cl2.

questions.question: The increasing order of ionic radii for given species is:
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
• In the case of cations,
    1. there is a decrease  in   ionic  radii   of the resulting  cation   as  compared to  its neutral  parent atom,  due to loss  of  electron /(s)
    2. greater the positive charge over the cation more would be a decrease in ionic size or radius.
  •  In the case of anion,
    1. the addition of each new electron (or negative charge) results in an increase in anionic radius.
    2. With an increase in negative charge over anion the anionic radius also becomes larger.
      Thus, considering as above the correct answer is-
      {tex}Al^{3+} \ {<} \ Mg^{2+} \ {<} \ Na^+ \ {<} \ F^- {<} O^{2-} \ {<} \ N^{3-}{/tex}

questions.question:
Al(3+) < Mg(2+) < Na+ < F- < O(2-) < N(3-)

options.option: the same number of electrons (9 electrons).
the same number of electrons (7 electrons).
the same number of electrons (8 electrons).

questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation: Isoelectronic species are elements or ions that have the same or an equal number of electrons. Although isoelectronic species have the same number of electrons, they are different in their physical and chemical properties. This Al(3+) < Mg(2+) < Na+ < F- < O(2-) < N(3-) trend is isoelectronic species.

than that of its parent neutral atom.

questions.question: The increasing order of bond dissociation energies of the following species is_____.
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
As bond order increases bond dissociation energy also increases.
Thus N2

  1. The triple bonded N2 with bond order =  3 has the highest bond dissociation energy.
  2. The doubly bonded O2 with  bond order = 2 has lesser bond dissociation energy than N2
  3. F2 and Cl2 both  are single bonded  having the same bond order = 1   

But, the electron-electron repulsive force (e-e repulsion) is maximum in the F2 molecule due to its small size. This accounts for the lower bond dissociation energy of  F2  as compared to Cl2.

questions.question: The relative tendency of an atom in a molecule to attract the shared pair of electrons towards itself is termed as its:
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation: A qualitative measure of the ability of an atom in a chemical compound to attract shared electrons to itself is called electronegativity. Unlike ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy, it is not a measurable quantity. However, a number of numerical scales of electronegativity of elements viz., Pauling scale, Mulliken-Jaffe scale, Allred-Rochow scale have been developed. The electronegativity of any given element is not constant; it varies depending on the element to which it is bound.

questions.question: The order of screening effect of electrons of s, p, d and f orbitals of a given shell of an atom on its outer shell electrons is:
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation: Screening effect order follows s>p>d>f, this due to the nature of the orbitals involved. s-orbitals are least diffused and the two electrons in s-orbitals provide greatest shielding of the nuclear charge to the outermost electron. The diffused character increases in the order p>d>f. f-orbitals are so diffused that fourteen electrons inside them provide virtually no screening of the nuclear charge.

questions.question: Select the species with diamagnetic nature:
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
{tex}{[Ni(CN]_4]}^{2-}{/tex} is a diamagnetic species because:

  1. The {tex}Ni^{2+}{/tex} ion has the valence shell electronic configuration: {tex}3d^8 , 4s^0{/tex} .
  2. The presence of a strong field {tex}CN^-{/tex} ions, as a legand forces all the electrons to get paired up in 3d  orbitals providing 4 pairs of paired electrons and one empty d shell. 
  3. The empty 3d, 3s, and two 4p orbitals undergo  dsp2 hybridization to get bonded up with [CN]- ion. Thus, [Ni(CN)4]2- shows a diamagnetic nature.

questions.question: The correct decreasing order of chemical reactivities in terms of their oxidizing properties for F, Cl, O and N is:
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
All these elements are p-block elements.
The oxidizing character of elements increases from left to right across a period because of the presence of vacant d - orbitals in their valence shells. Thus we get the decreasing order of oxidizing properties as F > O > N
Again, the oxidizing character of elements decreases down the group. Thus, we get F > Cl. However, the oxidizing character of O is more than that of Cl ie. O > Cl
Hence, the correct order of chemical reactivities of F, Cl, O, N in terms of their oxidizing properties is F > O > Cl > N.

questions.question:
Which of the following species are not known?
AgCl, PbI4, PCl5 , PbCl4
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
PbI4 does not exist because of its instability due to the following reasons:

  1. The tetrapositive lead ion (Pb4+) is unstable and gets easily reduced to more stable dipositive lead ion (Pb2+) due to the inert pair effect.
  2.  
    1. (Pb4+) being strong oxidant takes up the electron ( e-1) and would oxidize iodide ion (I-1) to iodine (I2).
    2. (Pb4+)  gets reduced to a more stable dipositive state (Pb2+).

Thus,  PbI4 does not exist due to its instability, and is, therefore, not known.

Since all the species have same number of electrons that is 10 , so they are isoelectronic.

questions.question:
The given set of elements are present in the 3rd period of the periodic table. Element X forms basic hydroxide which is used in the preparation of milk of magnesia and the element Y is a silvery white metal which is a good conductor of electricity. Find the largest and the smallest species in size.

  1. Element X
  2. Divalent cation of element X
  3. Element Y
  4. Trivalent cation of element Y
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation: The element X is magnesium. Its hydroxide Mg(OH)2 is used in the preparation of antacid (milk of magnesia). Its divalent cation is Mg2+. The element Y is Aluminium (Al) which is a silvery-white metal and it is a good conductor of electricity. Its trivalent cation is Al3+. Al is smaller than Mg due to the increased nuclear charge. Cations are smaller than their parent atoms. Therefore, Mg2+ is smaller than Mg and Al3+ is smaller than Al. Mg2+ and Al3+ are isoelectronic ions. Among isoelectronic species, the higher the positive charge, the smaller is the size of the ion. Therefore, the ionic radius of Al3+ is smaller than that of Mg2+.

questions.question: Which of the following species will have the largest size?
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation:
Atomic radii decrease across a period.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mg > Al
Cations are smaller than their parent atoms.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mg > Mg2+ and Al > Al3+
Now, Mg2+ and Al3+ are isoelectronic species.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mg2+ > Al3+
Thus, the largest species would be Mg.

questions.question: Which one of the elements with the following outer orbital configurations may exhibit largest number of oxidation states?
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation: All the given elements are d-block elements. For d-block element, it can lose its electrons from 3d and 4s subshell completely. Thus, the element which has higher number of electrons in 3d and 4s subshells exhibits largest number of oxidation states. Among the given options, 3d5, 4s2 has maximum number of electrons that is, 5 + 2 = 7. Hence, it has largest number of oxidation states.

questions.question:
Which of the following order is wrong?

  1. NH3 < PH3 < AsH3 - Acidic
  2. Li < Be < B < C - 1st ionization potential
  3. Al2O3 < MgO < Na2O < K2O - Basic
  4. Li+ < Na+ < K+ < Cs+ - Ionic radii
questions.topicName: Periodic Trends in Properties of Elements
questions.explanation: Ionization potential though increase on moving from left to right along a period but in case of Be and B the values are not according to this trend. The reason behind that is the completely filled valence orbital of Be which provides more stability and hence requires high ionization potential. In Be, the electronic configuration is 1s2 2s2 while for B is 1s2 2s2 2p1. Thus, 2s subshell is completely filled while 2p subshell in B is incomplete and less stable than that of electronic configuration of Be. Thus, the correct order is Li < B < Be < C.
